paper_title: NVIDIA OmniDreams: Real-Time Generative World Model for Closed-Loop Autonomous Vehicle Simulation

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: As autonomous vehicle capabilities advance, the safe evaluation of driving policies in long-tail scenarios remains a critical bottleneck. In closed-loop simulation, the driving policy model actively interacts with the environment, where its actions dynamically update the simulator state and directly influence the next set of generated sensor observations. While recent reconstruction-based neural simulators offer photorealism, they are fundamentally constrained by their initial captured data and struggle to generalize to highly dynamic or novel scenes. To overcome these limitations, we introduce OmniDreams, a foundation generative world model mid- and post-trained from the Cosmos diffusion model to autoregressively generate action-conditioned videos in real time. By leveraging the rich visual priors of Cosmos and mid- and post-training on 21k hours of driving scenarios, OmniDreams synthesizes complex, unobserved phenomena that are hard for traditional simulators to capture, such as extreme weather and unpredictable dynamic agent behaviors. Crucially, it autoregressively conditions its photorealistic sensor generation on past frames, the current simulator state, and immediate driving actions. Deployed in a closed-loop system with the Alpamayo 1 policy model and AlpaSim orchestrator, OmniDreams acts as a highly responsive, reactive environment, providing a scalable and comprehensive solution for training and evaluating next-generation autonomous driving policies. We additionally show preliminary results indicating that a world-action model (WAM) post-trained from OmniDreams achieves strong performance on the Physical AI Autonomous Vehicles NuRec dataset, surpassing the VLA-based Alpamayo 1.5 research policy model while using only 1/5 the total parameters. These results highlight the potential for a real-time world model like OmniDreams to also serve as a backbone for policy architectures.

sections.title: B Glossary
sections.content: 26 27 28 28 28 29 30 30 30 36 3 NVIDIA OmniDreams: Real-Time Generative World Model for Closed-Loop Autonomous Vehicle Simulation Figure 1: Closed-loop simulation workflow. policy model (here, Alpamayo 1 (NVIDIA, 2026)) or user sends an action to the AlpaSim simulation runtime. AlpaSim updates the simulation state and forwards the context to OmniDreams, which synthesizes the next camera frames and returns them to the policy, completing the loop. 1. Introduction Autonomous vehicle (AV) research is entering new phase. Reasoning-capable visionlanguageaction (VLA) models can think through complex situations, producing interpretable reasoning traces alongside planned trajectories (NVIDIA, 2026). Yet the core challenge remains: how to test these policies in safety-critical, long-tail scenarios before real-world deployment. Meeting this challenge requires simulator that is both interactive and scalable. In closed-loop evaluation, the policy actively drives within the simulator: it produces an action, the environment updates accordingly, and the policy receives the next sensor observations rendered from the updated state, shown in Fig. 1. This is essential because the policy actions influence how scenario unfolds over time. Recent progress in reconstruction-based neural simulation has significantly advanced AV development (NVIDIA, 2024). By reconstructing real-world scenes from captured data, these systems enable photorealistic whatif testing within environments grounded in reality. As result, developers can modify agent behaviors or trajectories and evaluate how policies respond under controlled variations of real scenarios. However, reconstruction-based workflows remain fundamentally anchored to the data that was originally observed. While they support many what-if scenarios within reconstructed scene, they struggle to scale beyond the captured corridor, introduce new scene content, or generate consistent photorealistic observations under substantially new conditions. In contrast, generative world models trained on massive amounts of visual data learn rich priors about how the world behaves and evolves visually. These priors allow such models to synthesize highly challenging phenomena, such as highly dynamic weather conditions (e.g., rain, storms, snow, and wind) or unusual, deformable objects (e.g., mattress on top of car) (Ren et al., 2025). They can also generate complex agent behaviors, including pedestrian behavior and articulated motion that rapidly change in response to the vehicles proximity or 4 NVIDIA OmniDreams: Real-Time Generative World Model for Closed-Loop Autonomous Vehicle Simulation stopping behavior. This technical report introduces NVIDIAs latest research on closed-loop simulation via generative world foundation model for autonomous driving. At the core of this research is new foundation model, OmniDreams, an action-conditioned generative world model that is midand post-trained on Cosmos (NVIDIA, 2025). OmniDreams generates photorealistic sensor observations while remaining interactive and responsive to the driving policy. OmniDreams includes the following key design concepts behind the workflow: operating as an autoregressive diffusion video generation-based simulator, which simulates in closedloop with the open-sourced Alpamayo 1 (NVIDIA, 2026) as the policy model and NVIDIA AlpaSim (NVIDIA, 2025) as the simulation orchestrator the KV cache stored from past generations is also attended during new frame generation, allowing

sections.title: OmniDreams to achieve long rollout consistency
sections.content: conditioning sensor generation on simulator state and driving actions real-time interactive rendering. The single-camera version of the 2B OmniDreams can render 68 FPS videos at 720p resolution (704 1280) with one GB300 GPU, whereas the 4-camera version of the 2B OmniDreams can render up to 105 FPS at 720p resolution with 16 GB300 GPUs Furthermore, we demonstrate that the internal representations learned by OmniDreams are inherently valuable for downstream driving tasks. Specifically, we show some preliminary results that policy model post-trained from OmniDreams improves performance on the PAI autonomous driving dataset, reducing collision from 6.9% to 4.2%, collision_front from 1.0% 0.9%, collision_lateral from 0.6% 0.4% and collision_rear from 5.3% 3.0%. Remarkably, it achieves this despite requiring roughly five times fewer parameters (2 vs. 10 B) than its VLA counterpart, Alpamayo 1.5 (NVIDIA, 2026). To the best of our knowledge, this is one of the first works to show that World-Action Models (WAMs) can offer superior solution to VLAs in autonomous driving, echoing recent findings in robotics (Ye et al., 2026). 2. Data Training closed-loop generative world model places specific demands on the data pipeline: the model must learn to synthesize photorealistic, temporally consistent sensor observations conditioned on structured scene representations, ego trajectories, and textual environment descriptions. This section describes the data sources, conditioning signal extraction, curation process, and final dataset composition used to midand post-train OmniDreams from the Cosmos-Predict 2.5 foundation model (NVIDIA, 2025). 2.1. Data Sources We use two AV datasets for training, Real Driving Scene (RDS) dataset used in (Agarwal et al., 2025) but containing seven camera views, and RDS-HQ-1M, newly curated and expanded version of RDS-HQ in (Ren et al., 2025) with 1.14𝑀 clips. Both datasets are sourced from real-world driving logs under diverse conditions in 15 countries in Europe, Asia, and the US. They consist of 1080p video clips recorded with 7 synchronized cameras (front-wide, front-telescope, front-left, front-right, rear-left, rear-right, rear-tele) at 30 frames per second. RDS contains 3M 20s clips, selected to balance several driving scenarios, e.g., rural vs. city environments, daytime/nighttime scenes, and slow/fast driving. We use this dataset for mid-training, and it is also included in the Cosmos-Predict 2.5-AV pre-training set. RDS-HQ-1M contains 1.14M clips, mix of 10s/20s scenes, with focus on quality and highly accurate world-scenario annotations (see below). We use this dataset for finetuning and post-training. 5 NVIDIA OmniDreams: Real-Time Generative World Model for Closed-Loop Autonomous Vehicle Simulation (a) Front-wide camera (b) Front-wide world scenario (abstract state) (c) Rear-tele camera (d) Rear-tele world scenario (abstract state) Figure 2: Example for world-scenario map rendering: lane lines (yellow), crosswalks (purple), cars (blue cuboids), and signs/signals (gray). 2.2. Conditioning Signal Extraction As described in Sec. 3, OmniDreams conditions generation on three input modalities: an abstract state of world-scenario map comprising rendered lane lines and bounding boxes which encodes the future ego trajectory, text prompt describing environmental conditions, and memory cache of recent visual history. Preparing paired training data, therefore, requires extracting these structured representations from raw driving logs. World-scenario map. The world-scenario map (abstract state) encodes both the high-definition map of the static scene and the dynamic actors that populate it. It also encodes the actions from the policy model or the user driver (in our real training data, actions come from test drivers). High-definition (HD) maps provide detailed and precise road annotations, including lane lines, road boundaries, stop lines, poles, crosswalks, road markings, traffic lights, and traffic signs. All map labels are obtained from pre-constructed city-level map and strictly matched to individual driving routes. We further perform 3D bounding-box detection and tracking of dynamic objects in each video clip, yielding 3D annotations for vehicles, pedestrians, and other vulnerable road users. 3D detection and tracking is run at 10 FPS and interpolated to 30 FPS to match the video frame rate. Then we follow the world-scenario map rendering scheme introduced in (NVIDIA, 2025). For each camera view, we render these elements as pixel-aligned lines, surfaces, and 2D-projected cuboids using camera intrinsics and extrinsics. The cuboids for road actors are shaded to distinguish heading and provide additional depth cues. An example world-scenario rendering is shown in Fig. 2. Text prompts. Text prompts enable control over environmental appearance such as weather, lighting, and time of day without modifying the underlying scene geometry. To generate text captions for training, we use VLM (Qwen2.5-VL7B (Bai et al., 2023)) to describe the video clips in our training sets. The VLM is tasked to describe the overall scenery, weather, driving behavior, and traffic. We perform captioning on 10s-long temporal windows and independently caption all 7 cameras. For each window, we generate three captions of varying lengths: short (40 words), medium (80 words), and long (200 words). These captions are sampled with probabilities 0.1/0.2/0.7 during model training to make the model robust to prompt length. As an example, this is one medium-length caption: The video depicts nighttime drive along well-lit urban road. The ego vehicle moves steadily forward, passing under bright streetlights that illuminate the surroundings. The road features clear white lane markings, including directional arrows indicating turns. Traffic signs, including yellow circular sign and blue rectangular sign, are visible at intervals, providing guidance for drivers. The environment includes patches of greenery, bushes, and trees lining the roadside, adding depth to the scene. billboard advertisement is also seen on the right side of the road. The road appears dry and in good condition. 2.3. Data Curation and Quality Filtering High data quality is critical for training world model that produces temporally consistent, artifact-free rollouts. We apply multi-stage filtering pipeline to remove problematic sequences before training. We discard any NVIDIA OmniDreams: Real-Time Generative World Model for Closed-Loop Autonomous Vehicle Simulation sequences with unreliable sensor data (e.g., jumps in the ego trajectory), unreliable annotations (e.g., high uncertainty in auto-labeled objects), or prediction disagreements. Next, we use VLM to detect undesirable visual artifacts in the camera data, such as chromatic aberration, and remove affected clips. As final step, we perform data de-duplication based on ego-trajectory and visual features to down-sample repetitive clips in the dataset, such as straight highway driving. 2.4. Dataset Composition OmniDreams training dataset spans 15 countries across North America, Europe, and Asia (see Tab. 1). RDS provides the mid-training data (16,600 h; 3M clips). Post-training and finetuning use RDS-HQ-1M (4,944 h; 1,142,285 clips), of which 504,488 are 10 clips (44%) and 637,797 are 20 clips (56%). We hold out 5,000 clips from the training set for evaluation and testing. Rather than sampling proportionally from the training distribution, these clips are balanced across various scenario categories covering road users (cyclists, pedestrians, motorcycles, strollers), large vehicles (trucks, trailers), weather and lighting (rain, snow, fog, night), and rare infrastructure (tunnels, railroad crossings, construction zones, accident scenes). For 300 of those clips, we additionally sourced longer (60 s) version, which are used for long-term temporal consistency evaluation. Tab. 1 summarizes the final training dataset. Table 1: Summary of the OmniDreams training dataset. Statistic Total driving hours (RDS) Total driving hours (RDS-HQ-1M) Number of sequences Frame resolution Camera views per frame Geographic regions Value 16,600 4,944 504,488 (10s) + 637,797 (20s) = 1,142,285 704 1280 7 in total, 4 in training 15 countries: US, DE, JP, KR, GB, FR, ES, SE, PT, DK, FI, PL, IT, AT, BE 2.5. Curation and Inspection with SIL-Wheel Building an OmniDreams training mixture and the matching held-out evaluation sets requires reasoning about millions of candidate clips along multiple axes: geography, weather, time of day, ego behavior, agent density, and rare-but-safety-critical scenarios such as collisions or vulnerable road users. We use NVIDIA SILs video search and curation platform, SIL-Wheel (NVIDIA, 2026), as the workbench for this selection. SIL-Wheel indexes the underlying corpora (including RDS and RDS-HQ-1M) at clip granularity and exposes set of composable search primitives, spanning caption search, dense semantic and visual retrieval, ego-trajectory queries, classifier scoring, and perception-based filters, that can be combined within single query. For OmniDreams, SIL-Wheel plays three concrete roles: Finetuning slice construction. We compose post-training mixtures by retrieving clips that match conditions that the natural-data distribution under-represents, including rare weather, construction zones, vulnerable road users, articulated motion, and complex multi-agent interactions, and upweighting those slices in the post-training corpus. Eval-set slice construction. The held-out evaluation set described in Sec. 2.4 is decomposed into sliceaware buckets through the same search interface. This enables the long-tail and per-scenario reporting in Sec. 9, alongside aggregate metrics. Data inspection. The clip-centric UI is used to validate autolabel quality on RDS-HQ-1M, triage clips surfaced by the quality-filtering pipeline of Sec. 2.3, and visually verify that targeted slices contain what we intended before they enter the training mixture. NVIDIA OmniDreams: Real-Time Generative World Model for Closed-Loop Autonomous Vehicle Simulation Figure 3: OmniDreams conditions on three inputs: text prompt, the next abstract state from the simulator, and history-frame cache to generate next-step sensor frames that are returned to the policy in closed loop. In the first generation step, OmniDreams also conditions on single RGB image, which is not shown in this figure. 3. Model Architecture key requirement for closed-loop simulation is interactivity, allowing the model to respond immediately to changes in actions from the driving policy model or human input. To support this, OmniDreams performs generation in an autoregressive manner. At each time step, the simulator updates the world state based on the latest action, and the model generates short sequence of future frames conditioned on this updated state. This design differs from offline video generation methods, such as (OpenAI, 2024; NVIDIA, 2025; DeepMind, 2025; Wan et al., 2025; Polyak et al., 2024), which produce long clips using bidirectional and diffusion-based sampling. Instead, OmniDreams adopts causal diffusion formulation, in which each prediction depends only on past observations and the current conditioning. Temporal consistency is achieved through streaming KV cache. By reusing previously computed attention keys and values, the model maintains long-term context without recomputing the full sequence, enabling efficient and stable rollouts over extended horizons. We provide two variants of OmniDreams: (1) Single-view model (OmniDreams-SV): Generates single frontfacing camera view, producing 8 frames per step (2 latent frames); (2) Multi-view model (OmniDreams-MV): Jointly generates four synchronized views (front camera, cross-left camera, cross-right camera, front-telescope camera), producing 16 frames per step (4 latent frames). 3.1. Network Architecture OmniDreams conditions on the following inputs, shown in Fig. 3: First-frame RGB: The first frame of the simulation session, encoded as clean latent tokens to initialize the generation 8 NVIDIA OmniDreams: Real-Time Generative World Model for Closed-Loop Autonomous Vehicle Simulation Text prompt: high-level description of environmental attributes such as lighting, weather, and time of day. The text is encoded using the Cosmos text encoder and injected via cross-attention layers Abstract world scenario: structured representation of the simulator state that is aligned to the video being generated by OmniDreams, including maps (e.g., lane lines, traffic poles) and dynamic agents represented as bounding boxes (see Fig. 1). The static maps and dynamic agent trajectories are obtained in advance (such as via real recordings from vehicle), and the driving action produced by the policy model or human driver is used to render the world-scenario conditioning video input Memory cache: streaming KV cache storing previously generated tokens for temporal context The first-frame latent and conditioning signals are combined with noisy latent tokens, then processed by causal transformer backbone similar to that of the Cosmos-Predict 2.5 base model (NVIDIA, 2025). OmniDreams incorporates lightweight control branch to efficiently inject structured world conditioning into the generative model. Instead of processing control inputs with separate network (e.g., ControlNet), the structured simulator state is first encoded using small MLP into compact control tokens. These tokens are aligned with the latent representation and concatenated with the visual tokens before being fed into the transformer. This design introduces minimal computational overhead while enabling effective conditioning on scene structure. It also stabilizes autoregressive generation by maintaining clear separation between control signals and visual content. In practice, the lightweight control branch allows efficient integration of the simulator state while preserving the throughput required for real-time generation. 3.2. Consistent Multi-View Generation OmniDreams supports joint generation of multiple camera views while maintaining cross-view consistency and real-time performance. Each camera view is associated with learnable embedding that is added to the token representation to indicate the view identity. In addition, each view has its own conditioning inputs, including text prompt, the first-frame RGB image, and structured world state corresponding to its camera configuration. This allows the model to represent both shared scene structure and view-specific appearance. naive multi-view formulation applies full self-attention across all tokens over time and views, resulting in quadratic complexity 𝒪(𝑁 2𝑇 2) with respect to the number of views 𝑁 and temporal length 𝑇 . This quickly becomes impractical for real-time simulation. To address this, OmniDreams factorizes attention into two components: Temporal attention is applied independently within each view. Tokens attend to previous frames through the causal KV cache, capturing motion dynamics and maintaining temporal consistency. Cross-view attention is applied across views at each time step. Tokens from different views attend to one another, enabling consistent representation of shared scene elements such as geometry, object positions, and motion. Fig. 4 gives summary of our OmniDreams-MV. This factorization reduces the overall complexity to: significantly improving efficiency compared to full joint attention. 𝒪(𝑁 𝑇 2) + 𝒪(𝑁 2), This design enables scalable multi-view generation with strong cross-view consistency, while supporting higher resolution, longer temporal context, and more views. In practice, OmniDreams supports up to 7 synchronized views for surround-view simulation. 9 NVIDIA OmniDreams: Real-Time Generative World Model for Closed-Loop Autonomous Vehicle Simulation Figure 4: Multi-view OmniDreams DiT. Compared to the single-view Cosmos-Predict 2.5 backbone, each MultiView Cross Block adds (i) per-view view embedding that is summed with the time embedding and used as an additive AdaLN signal on the shift, scale, and gate of all sublayers, and (ii) Cross-View Attention sublayer after the text Cross-Attention that attends across the tokens of all views to enforce cross-camera consistency. 4. Training We employ multi-stage training strategy to efficiently leverage diverse data sources in building OmniDreams-SV and OmniDreams-MV. From the bidirectional video generation model, Cosmos-Predict 2.5, we first mid-train it on the RDS dataset to improve its AV capability, and then add another mid-training stage that adapts the model for bidirectional multi-view generation by incorporating cross-view attention architecture (for OmniDreams-MV only). This model is then adapted for autoregressive generation via Diffusion Forcing mid-training stage. Controllable generation via world-scenario map control is then added in post-training stages to both the bidirectional and autoregressive models. Lastly, distillation stage with Self Forcing DMD enables few-step generation while removing autoregressive error accumulation. After our training, we can roll out minutes-long videos without significantly sacrificing quality, shown in Fig. 5 at inference time. 4.1. World-Scenario Control and Multi-view adaptation Preamble Following (NVIDIA, 2025), OmniDreams-MV uses the rectified-flow (Liu et al., 2022) objective to train the model. Formally, let denote latent video, 𝜖 𝒩 (0, 𝐼) noise sampled from the standard normal distribution, and 𝑡 [0, 1] time-step drawn from logit-normal distribution. The flow matching objective is 𝐿 = Ex,𝑡[u𝜃(x𝑡, 𝑡) v𝑡2], where x𝑡 = (1 𝑡)x + 𝑡𝜖 and v𝑡 = 𝜖 x. The model can also be conditioned on auxiliary information c, denoted as u𝜃(x𝑡, 𝑡; c), which includes text caption, first-frame images, and control signals. Multi-view adaptation OmniDreams-MV supports generating videos from front-wide, cross-left, cross-right, and front-telescope camera sensors. These videos (particularly the cross-left, cross-right, and front-telescope) have unique intrinsics and 10 NVIDIA OmniDreams: Real-Time Generative World Model for Closed-Loop Autonomous Vehicle Simulation Figure 5: Autoregressive video generation diagram (right) comparing bidirectional image-to-video denoising (left) and causal KV-cache based generation for consistent long video rollouts. motion that are rarely encountered in general-domain videos. Hence, mid-training stage is needed to bestow the model with knowledge of multi-view videos. Starting from Cosmos-Predict 2.5, we enable learnable view embeddings  (Fig. 4)  and train the model on uniform mix of front-wide, cross-left, cross-right, and fronttelescope clips. This is followed by adding cross-view attention layers to the network and training on multi-view videos, during which the cross-view attention layers learn cross-view correspondences and consistency. View embedding is injected into the tokens through adaptive layer normalization in each transformer block, similar to how timestep embedding is injected. These view embeddings are zero-initialized to ensure stable convergence when they are added to the network. Cross-view attention layers are added to each block after the cross-attention layer and before the MLPs. The output projection weights of each cross-view attention layer are also zero-initialized for stable convergence. We train on 1:1 mixture of text-to-video and image-to-video tasks to support first-frame conditioning while preserving the models capacity to generate novel content. World-scenario map control world-scenario map is needed in both OmniDreams-SV and OmniDreams-MV for the video generation models to function as world simulation models. Using pre-trained or multi-view-adapted weights, we append worldscenario control branch initialized to zero and train with the flow-matching objective. For training efficiency, we first train on 93-frame clips until convergence, then expand to 189-frame clips to learn longer-term temporal consistency. The result of this training is World-Scenario controlled bidirectional models, which are suitable as teacher models for further distillation. 4.2. Mid-training for Autoregressive Generation We apply causal masking with Diffusion Forcing (Chen et al., 2024) training to convert the bidirectional models into causal models for autoregressive generation. Empirically, we found that this training benefited from extensive optimization beyond the scale of the available World-Scenario labeled data. Hence, we perform this training from model weights without world-scenario control on the RDS dataset, and then add the control branch and continue Diffusion Forcing training using the RDS-HQ-1M dataset. Causal Masking Let x1:𝑇 denote latent video of 𝑇 latent frames. Distillation To enable few-step generation while eliminating the compounding errors that arise in long autoregressive rollouts, we apply Self Forcing (Huang et al., 2025), training framework that combines autoregressive selfrollout with holistic, video-level distribution-matching objective based on Distribution Matching Distillation (DMD) (Yin et al., 2024). Self Forcing Training via Self-Rollout. Standard teacher forcing trains diffusion models by conditioning each frames denoising on clean, ground-truth context frames. At inference time, however, the model must condition on its own previously generated (and thus imperfect) outputs, creating distribution mismatch that causes compounding errors over long rolloutsa phenomenon known as exposure bias (Huang et al., 2025). Self Forcing (Huang et al., 2025) closes this train-test gap by replacing ground-truth context with self-generated frames during training: the model performs an autoregressive self-rollout, generating each frame conditioned on its own previously generated outputs via 𝐾-step diffusion process (𝐾=2, with timestep schedule of [1000, 450] in our implementation). To keep training tractable, gradient backpropagation is restricted to single randomly sampled denoising step 𝑠 Uniform(1, . . . , 𝑇 ) per training iteration, ensuring that all intermediate steps receive supervision across iterations. Gradients are additionally detached from the KV cache embeddings of previous frames, confining gradient flow to the current frame. Long-video generation is supported via rolling KV cache (Huang et al., 2025): we maintain fixed-size cache of the most recent 𝐿 frames, evicting the oldest entry whenever new frame is added. This reduces inference complexity from 𝒪(𝑇 𝐿2) to 𝒪(𝑇 𝐿) and enables generation of arbitrarily long sequences. To match inference behavior during training, we restrict attention to the same rolling window. Holistic Distribution Matching via DMD. Rather than supervising individual denoised frames with pixel-wise reconstruction loss, Self Forcing employs holistic, video-level distribution matching objective. Concretely, it minimizes the reverse KL divergence between the distribution of self-rolled-out video clips 𝑝𝜃 and the real data distribution 𝑝data, using the DMD formulation (Yin et al., 2024): ℒDMD(𝜃) = [ 1 ˆ𝑥 sg[ˆ𝑥 (f𝜓(ˆ𝑥𝑡, 𝑡) f𝜑(ˆ𝑥𝑡, 𝑡))] 2] , (2) where ˆ𝑥 is the self-rolled-out video clip, f𝜑 is frozen real score network, f𝜓 is learned fake score network trained to estimate the score of the generated distribution, and sg[] denotes the stop-gradient operation. The score difference f𝜓 f𝜑 provides gradient that steers the generator toward the real data manifold at the full-sequence level, without requiring paired data or pixel-wise supervision. Progressive Training with Longer Teacher. While the Self Forcing distillation procedure above substantially reduces exposure bias for short rollouts, we observe that the resulting model still exhibits shifting artifactstemporal inconsistencies that accumulate 12 NVIDIA OmniDreams: Real-Time Generative World Model for Closed-Loop Autonomous Vehicle Simulation when the rolling KV cache extends beyond the training context window during long generation. To mitigate this, we adopt progressive teacher strategy. We first train bidirectional (non-causal) video model with substantially longer temporal context window; as bidirectional model, it does not suffer from rolling-cache artifacts. We then use this long-context bidirectional model as the teacher to continue finetuning the model that was previously distilled with the short-context teacher. As shown in Fig. 9, this progressive strategy significantly reduces shifting artifacts in long autoregressive rollouts. For the distillation stage, we use high-quality and challenging subset from our full training corpus with 58𝑘 videos. Concentrating distillation on this subset allows OmniDreams to better adapt to the perceptually demanding conditions of complex urban environments such as dense traffic, varied lighting, and intricate road geometry while retaining generation quality on highway and rural scenarios. 5. Training-free Model Inference Optimization This section describes the inference system that runs the few-step distilled Cosmos-Predict 2.5 backbone (Sec. 4) in our closed-loop simulator. Everything below is training-free: no diffusion-model weights are touched relative to the distilled checkpoint. The deployed system generates 16-frame four-view chunk at 7041280 in 151 ms on 16-GPU NVIDIA GB300 cabinet (105 effective FPS per camera) and an 8-frame single-view chunk in 118 ms on single GB300 (68 effective FPS). 5.1. Model Optimization Local temporal attention. Full temporal attention over an unbounded rollout is impractical for both compute and cache size. We use local-window attention with fixed window of 6 latent frames (24 RGB frames) for OmniDreams-SV and 8 latent frames (32 RGB frames) for OmniDreams-MV. The window size is tradeoff between memory and speed. Streaming static-shape KV cache. The DiT is autoregressive: each chunk reuses keys/values from previous chunks. We pre-allocate the cache to the fixed size and keep its tensor shape static across the whole rollout, and move cache updates on separate thread to allow the main thread free from cache update latency. Compile and CUDA Graphs. Because the KV cache is pre-allocated to fixed local-attention window (Sec. 5.1), the DiT forward runs on tensors of single static shape for the entire rollout. We compile it with torch.compile (Ansel et al., 2024) and capture it into CUDA Graph (NVIDIA, 2024); the graph is captured lazily on the first chunk and reused for every subsequent chunk and every rollout of the same shape. Lightweight encoders and decoders. We swap the original VAE used in Cosmos with LightVAE or LightTAE from LightX2V (LightX2V, 2025). LightVAE encodes the conditioning video (the initial frame and the world-scenario condition videos, Sec. 2) into latent space for OmniDreams-SV and offers the best balance between reconstruction quality and speed. OmniDreamsMV uses pixel shuffle technique to replace LightVAE to further speed up the model. Its latency is negligible. We use LightTAE to decode the denoised latents back to RGB for both OmniDreams-MV and OmniDreams-SV and is the best for its extremely low decode latency. Hoisting step-invariant operators. We hoist step-invariant computations out of the denoising loop. Self-attention at every denoising step uses the same RoPE frequencies, so we compute them once per chunk instead of once per step. Likewise, the patchify and unpatchify operators that convert latents into and out of the DiTs token layout depend only on the chunk shape, so we run them once at the start and end of chunk generation rather than on every entry and exit of the DiT. Multi-GPU Inference The DiT exposes three natural parallelism axes: the camera-view axis 𝑉 (at most the number of cameras), the temporal axis 𝑇 within chunk (at most the number of latent frames per chunk), and the spatial axis 𝐻𝑊 inside one frames attention. We use hierarchical context-parallel strategy that shards along all three. Our 16-GPU four-view configuration is 𝑉 =4, 𝑇 =4, 𝐻𝑊 =1. Ranks are added in the order 𝑉 𝑇 𝐻𝑊 , chosen by how each axis interacts with attention cost. Selfattention dominates the DiT and is per-camera, so sharding along 𝑉 never splits self-attention call: 4 ranks along 𝑉 give close-to-linear speed-up in our 4-camera setting and saturate this axis at 4 GPUs. Beyond that, the remaining work sits inside one camera, leaving 𝑇 or 𝐻𝑊 . We prefer 𝑇 because temporal sharding leaves the per-time-step cross-view attention block intact, whereas 𝐻𝑊 sharding splits both selfand cross-view attention. With 4 latent frames per chunk in four-view inference, 𝑉 𝑇 = 44 = 16 already saturates 16 GPUs with 𝐻𝑊 =1, and we would only start sharding 𝐻𝑊 beyond 16 GPUs. For the context-parallel attention itself we use an in-house ring-attention implementation (Liu et al., 2024) that overlaps KV-shard transfer with local attention compute. 5.3. FlashDreams: Generalized Inference and Serving Infra The recipes above, including streaming static-shape KV cache, local-window attention, CUDA-graph capture, is not specific to our model architecture. We have packaged it into FlashDreams (NVIDIA, 2026), an open-source streaming inference stack for autoregressive world and video models, and validated it on additional Wan2.1based backbones (Wan et al., 2025). On Self Forcing (Huang et al., 2025), Wan2.1-based autoregressive text-to-video model, FlashDreams reaches up to 1.95 speed-up over the official implementation on single GB200. On Lingbot-World (Team et al., 2026), Wan2.1-14B-based autoregressive camera-control world model, it reaches up to 2.49 speed-up on 4 H100s. Beyond the per-chunk model optimizations, FlashDreams also ships the end-to-end serving stack described in Sec. 6: gRPC and webRTC server/client protocol that streams control inputs from the client and RGB frames back from the server. We hope FlashDreams serves as practical, extensible starting point for the community to deploy autoregressive world and video models in latency-critical settings, and we welcome contributions to grow the set of supported backbones. 6. Closed-Loop Simulation Integration For closed-loop use, the video model is packaged into stateful video model server that talks to simulator client over gRPC. Fig. 6 shows two consecutive round-trips. In each round-trip, the client sends trajectory from the policy model, text prompt, and an initial frame (only on the very first chunk); the server renders the world-scenario conditioning, runs two denoising steps of causal DiT with streaming KV cache, decodes the latents to RGB, and returns JPEG-encoded frames. The client then advances its world state, queries its driving policy for the next trajectory, and sends the next request. The server is stateful for the whole rollout: the KV cache, captured CUDA Graphs, and renderer state are all reused chunk-to-chunk, so each follow-up request carries only the new trajectory and prompt. This poses unique challenges, stemming from three main properties of video models as renderers: 15 NVIDIA OmniDreams: Real-Time Generative World Model for Closed-Loop Autonomous Vehicle Simulation Distributed inference for high frame rates, video models are run with multiple processes (typically one per GPU) and use networking for inter-process communication. This precludes the standard in-process integration of video models (e.g. as Python dependency), as that would require the simulation layer to become distributed as well. Autoregressive nature video models are naturally internally stateful, preventing rendering frames in an arbitrary order. This requires the simulator to keep track of that state and avoid invalidation. Chunk-based generation for performance and quality reasons, the current generation of video models generates frames in chunks, which correspond to underlying VAE temporal compression. This implies quantizing time into intervals over which the agents behavior must be fixed and known at the start of each interval. In the sections below, we describe how we approach each of these issues in our prototype integration with NVIDIA AlpaSim (NVIDIA, 2025), research-oriented AV simulator. 6.1. AlpaSim NVIDIA AlpaSim is an open-source, research-oriented AV simulator. To maximize software modularity and deployment flexibility, AlpaSim adopts microservice architecture: its core runtime is lightweight and uses remote procedure calls (RPCs) to specialized services, such as camera frame renderers, AV policy, and physics simulation, via the gRPC protocol. The entire system is deployed as set of Docker containers, allowing maximum flexibility in Python and OS environments, as well as deployment (including hosting different components in different data centers). AlpaSim is built with throughput in mind. Thanks to its use of asynchronous RPC requests, launching multiple concurrent rollouts allows Pythons event loop to dynamically serialize them across microservices, automatically implementing pipeline parallelism. This imposes requirement on individual services to keep track of their state across multiple, potentially out-of-order, rollout requests. Our integration of OmniDreams involves replacing NVIDIA NuRec as the camera frame renderer, defining new gRPC protocols to manage the rendering state, and making AlpaSims runtime loop more flexible to accommodate the chunk semantics of video models. 6.2. Networked integration in AlpaSim The standard approach to running multi-GPU deployments spanning multiple nodes is rank-based parallelism: the same Python script is executed in parallel (usually once per GPU), with environment variables indicating its rank, the total number of processes, and the address of the master process. The script contains conditional statements which influence its behavior, e.g. restricting each rank to processing subrange of all video tokens. This pattern does not naturally extend to using the video model as subcomponent in larger system, e.g. simulator which contains state-keeping logic to be executed only once and other submodules (such as the AV policy or traffic simulation) which may want to launch with their own rank-parallel logic. Our solution is to add second layer to the networking hierarchy via gRPC. We implement gRPC server running on rank 0 of the video model deployment that receives rendering requests and forwards them to the remaining ranks via NCCL events. Once rendering chunk is complete, the generated frames are gathered on rank 0, serialized, and sent back to AlpaSim. This solution avoids unifying the dependencies, code, and rank dispatch logic of OmniDreams with the remaining components of AlpaSim. Future work will improve this scheme by using RDMA-based gRPC implementations and/or treating gRPC as just coordination layer for synchronizing bulk video frame transfers between policy and OmniDreams over NCCL. This will remove the complexity and lossiness of frame encoding and decoding, shifting the burden to high-throughput datacenter networks. 16 NVIDIA OmniDreams: Real-Time Generative World Model for Closed-Loop Autonomous Vehicle Simulation 6.3. Session-based state OmniDreams uses the appearance of previously generated video frames to guide the appearance of subsequent ones, enabling high-quality generation of richly dynamic worlds. Internally, this is equivalent to the OmniDreams KV cache state. At the integration level, we abstract it as sessions each rollout starts with new session request, which includes the (seed) first frame of simulation and the map representation of the world. The OmniDreams server generates fresh ID for the session and associates it with the received inputs and pre-allocated KV cache. It returns the ID to the AlpaSim client, which then includes it in all its requests, indicating which rollout state each request corresponds to. This is an established pattern already in use for other stateful services, such as policy and traffic simulation. 6.4. Supporting chunked generation Each frame of video generated in single autoregressive step is conditioned on world-scenario render (see Sec. 2.2 and Fig. 2). As the generation is chunked, the simulator needs to provide the ego and actor poses for all frames in the chunk when making the request. This is challenging because the policy and traffic models cannot mutate these trajectories mid-way through video chunk without invalidating the entire video chunk. While the goal for future generations of world models is to reduce the chunk size and eventually achieve frame-at-a-time generation, we have considered two strategies for handling the current chunk-based semantics and have implemented one in AlpaSim. 6.4.1. Post-fetch generation One strategy, termed post-fetch generation, is to roll out the policy and traffic models throughout video model chunk without providing the policy with new visual inputs. This requires the policy to support generation when the last available frame lags behind the last available ego position, property common in production AV stacks but less so in research models. Once the policy and traffic calls within the chunk have been rolled out, video-model request is made using the freshly generated trajectories. The response video frames logically occur before the video model request; hence, injecting them into the simulation timeline puts the timeline out of order. Because video chunk fetch occurs after advancing the simulation timer, we call this strategy post-fetch. 6.4.2. Pre-fetch generation In AlpaSim, instead of requiring out-of-sync video & egomotion processing, we choose to have single pass of the policy and traffic models generate multi-step trajectories, rather than just single-step next-pose prediction. This also applies to physics and controller modules that operate at the trajectory level rather than the pose level. In the simulation loop, we enforce that both services make their predictions at the same timestamps as the video model chunk boundaries, with higher priority. Each time new chunk starts, we append the newly generated trajectories to the egos and the actors historical trajectories, committing to follow them until the next chunk starts. We then interpolate those trajectories at instants corresponding to video frames and send request to the video model. We convert each frame in response to an event that injects it into the rollout state at the precise timestamp when it would logically be captured. We call this strategy pre-fetch because the video model call happens before advancing the simulation timer. The choice of pre-fetch for AlpaSim is mainly to preserve the ordering of events, which could lead to elusive bugs if violated. 7. Post-training OmniDreams as World-Action Model (WAM) complementary question is whether the representations OmniDreams learns during generative pre-training are themselves useful for driving: can the same backbone be repurposed into competitive policy? We fine-tune the single-view checkpoint (OmniDreams-SV, 2 parameters, 120 front-wide camera) into an end-to-end trajectory predictor and compare it against Alpamayo 1.5 (NVIDIA, 2026) inside the same AlpaSim closedloop stack. Because this task does not require world-scenario map conditioning control, we initialize our NVIDIA OmniDreams: Real-Time Generative World Model for Closed-Loop Autonomous Vehicle Simulation policy prediction training using an OmniDreams causal autoregressive checkpoint prior to world-scenario map finetuning. The policy predicts 6.4-second future ego trajectory at 10 Hz (64 waypoints). 7.1. Architecture and training The OmniDreams causal DiT backbone is kept structurally identical, with two additions per frame. First, patch features from DINOv2 (dinov2_vitb14) (Oquab et al., 2023)-initialized encoder and from 30 front-telescope camera are passed through learnable linear projections and summed into the noisy video latent tokens as additional conditioning. Second, history token (embedding the previous 1.6 of ego motion via small MLP) is appended to each frames token set and interleaved with the video patches along the temporal axis. The attention mask preserves the original video-to-video causality (a video token attends only to current and past video tokens, exactly as during generative pre-training) and extends it to history tokens with one-way coupling: each history token attends to all current and past video tokens but not to any other history token. Let ℎ denote the DiT output at history-token position. This ℎ is fed to 12-layer U-Net-shaped MLP that parameterizes flow matching velocity field u𝜑(𝜏𝑠, 𝑠; ℎ) over the future-trajectory latent 𝜏 R643, using the same flow matching formulation as the video backbone (Sec. 4). We train with joint denoising objective summing flow matching losses on the video and trajectory latents, with independently sampled flow-matching times (per-frame 𝑡 for video, per-sequence 𝑠 for trajectory) so that the history token must extract information at whatever fidelity of visual context is currently available, rather than co-adapting to single noise level. At inference we drop the interleaved-noise schedule and run single causal forward pass: 4 video latent frames with small noise, one history token, and 4 flow-matching steps to sample the trajectory. Since we only care about predicting the future trajectory, no video denoising is needed, so the DiT backbone runs only once and the 4 denoising steps are confined to the lightweight trajectory MLP. 7.2. Closed-loop comparison to Alpamayo 1.5 We evaluate the same OmniDreams WAM checkpoint under the original Alpamayo 1.5 protocol: 10 Hz replanning rate and 20-second rollouts over 574-scene Physical AI Autonomous Vehicles NuRec dataset (NVIDIA, 2025). The subsetting here is only done to exclude scenes on which OmniDreams WAM was trained on. Compared to Alpamayo 1.5, the OmniDreams-finetuned policy reduces Collision from 6.9% to 4.2% (Front: 1.0% to 0.9%, Lateral: 0.6% to 0.4%, and Rear:5.3% to 3.0%), despite using roughly five times fewer parameters (2 vs. 10 B). The same backbone that synthesizes next-frame camera observations therefore encodes scene state at fidelity sufficient to outperform dedicated, much larger policy. World-modelbased policies have previously been explored in domains such as robotic manipulation (Ye et al., 2026), and here we show that the same paradigm has the potential to transfer to drivinga setting where the future is highly multimodal, the safety bar is high, and policies must generalize across long tail of real-world traffic situations. For autonomous driving in particular, this suggests that the OmniDreams backbone need not remain passive sensor simulator wrapped around an external driving stack: the same model can act as the shared backbone of the driving policy itself, and we see joint world-modelpolicy training for AV, where one model learns to both render and drive through real-world traffic, as promising direction for future work. Sec. 9.4 and Fig. 12 show additional results on cross-policy comparison in both Nurec and OmniDreams simulators. 8. Post-Training OmniDreams as Diffusion Fixer Beyond serving as standalone generative sensor simulator, OmniDreams can also be used to improve existing reconstruction-based simulation systems. Neural reconstruction methods, including NeRFs and 3D Gaussian Splatting, provide an effective way to build simulation environments directly from real-world sensor data and have become practical foundation for autonomous-driving simulation. However, these systems remain sensitive to novel-view extrapolation. When the requested camera pose deviates substantially from the original capture trajectory, reconstructed renderings can exhibit blurred details, missing content, ghosting, floating 18 NVIDIA OmniDreams: Real-Time Generative World Model for Closed-Loop Autonomous Vehicle Simulation Figure 7: Reconstruction artifact correction. The first row shows input frames rendered by reconstruction-based simulator, which can contain novel-view artifacts such as blur, ghosting, missing regions, and spurious geometry. The second row shows the corresponding OmniDreams-corrected outputs. The post-trained OmniDreams model removes reconstruction artifacts while preserving the scene layout, camera viewpoint, and driving-relevant structures. geometry, and other view-dependent artifacts. These artifacts can degrade the realism of closed-loop rollouts and may introduce undesirable distribution shifts for downstream policies. To address this limitation, we post-train distilled autoregressive OmniDreams checkpoint for reconstructionartifact correction. We construct paired training data consisting of degraded rendering from reconstructionbased simulator and corresponding clean target images. Inspired by prior works (Wu et al., 2025; de Lutio et al., 2026; Zhang et al., 2026), during post-training, rather than starting the denoising process from random Gaussian noise, we start the denoising process from the degraded rendering itself, following the formulation used in prior works. In this setting, OmniDreams learns flow that maps artifact-corrupted reconstruction outputs onto the clean-image manifold while preserving the scene layout, camera viewpoint, and driving-relevant structure. At inference time, this post-trained model can be applied as an autoregressive correction module on top of pre-reconstructed scenes. Given rendered frame from the reconstruction system, OmniDreams refines the image by removing novel-view artifacts while maintaining temporal consistency through its causal history and KV-cache conditioning. This enables reconstruction-based simulators to retain their strengths in scene-specific geometric grounding, while benefiting from the learned visual priors of generative world model. Experiments and Results To be effective, generative closed-loop simulator must accurately model real-world behavior and appearance while maintaining simulation capabilities that extend beyond those of standard neural reconstruction approaches. This section details how OmniDreams delivers on this premise across four main axes: generating high-quality appearance and realistic dynamics, producing stable rollouts over extended horizons, enabling the controlled generation of long-tail scenarios, and facilitating real-time closed-loop policy evaluation. 9.1. Long Rollouts Stable long-horizon generation is enabled by three design choices introduced earlier: causal autoregressive formulation with streaming KV cache (Sec. 3), Self Forcing distillation with progressive long-context teacher to suppress exposure bias and shifting artifacts (Sec. 4), and bounded local-attention window with attention-sink tokens (tokens from the first RGB frame) at inference time. Together, these let OmniDreams recondition to an updated state and recent history at every step, while avoiding appearance drift, identity loss, and the accumulation of artifacts that typically affect autoregressive video models over long rollouts. Long-tail Coverage and Counterfactual Variations OmniDreams provides two complementary handles for accessing scenarios that are rare or impossible to capture in fleet logs. First, the prompt and first frame-driven conditioning enables single trained checkpoint to generate broad variations in environments, agents, and ego behavior without retraining (Sec. 9.3.1). Second, lightweight targeted finetuning steers the model toward distributions that are otherwise too rare to emerge from natural data sampling (Sec. 9.3.2). At scale, both handles turn the simulator into data engine for balanced training and evaluation sets with broad scenario coverage. 9.3.1. Controllable Scenario Editing The conditioning interfaces introduced in Sec. 3 expose two channels for counterfactual editing: the text prompt, which controls environmental attributes such as weather, lighting, and time of day (Wu et al., 2025); and the abstract world-scenario map (lane geometry and bounding boxes), which controls scene structure and agent layout. Together with the first-frame RGB seed, these channels allow developer to systematically vary what the policy sees while keeping unspecified attributes stable across variants. Fig. 10 illustrates these controls on single seed scene. By editing the conditioning signals such as the first frame, text prompt, and abstract world-scenario map, we enable targeted scenario variations. These edits allow users to evaluate the policys behavior in different scenarios. Across these variants, attributes that are not targeted by the edit, including road geometry, static scene structure, and distant background content, remain visually stable. 9.3.2. Out-of-Distribution Object Modeling Beyond modifying weather, lighting, ego motion, and in-distribution traffic participants, OmniDreams can also be adapted to model objects that are out-of-distribution relative to standard autonomous-driving logs. This capability is important for stress-testing policies under unusual visual conditions, such as the sudden appearance of uncommon animals, oversized objects, or other rare obstacles that are difficult to collect in real-world fleet data at scale. naive approach is to directly edit the first-frame RGB image by inserting out-of-distribution objects and then generate the full video. However, this often leads to visual artifacts and inconsistent dynamics, as the inserted objects are not represented in the world-scenario map and therefore conflict with the dynamic cuboid in the world-scenario map conditioning used by the model. As result, the model receives inconsistent signals: the RGB image suggests the presence of an object, while the structured conditioning provides no corresponding position, extent, or trajectory. This mismatch makes it difficult for the model to reconcile the edited appearance with the simulator state over time. To improve flexibility, we further post-train another variant of OmniDreams with randomized dynamic-cuboid dropout. During this stage, cuboids representing dynamic agents are randomly removed from the abstract NVIDIA OmniDreams: Real-Time Generative World Model for Closed-Loop Autonomous Vehicle Simulation Figure 10: Controllable scenario editing. Starting from given scene, OmniDreams supports targeted edits by modifying its conditioning inputs: text prompts can be used to modify appearance attributes such as weather and time of day of the first frame condition, and by also adapting the abstract world-scenario map, and ego-state conditioning changes the driven trajectory. Unedited attributes such as road geometry and distant scene context remain visually stable across variants. world-scenario map, while static cuboids, such as parked vehicles, are left unchanged. This prevents the model from relying exclusively on dynamic cuboids to determine the location and motion of every moving object. Instead, the model learns to infer plausible object persistence and motion from visual history, the first-frame seed, and surrounding scene context. At inference time, this allows out-of-distribution objects inserted into the initial frame to propagate more naturally through the generated video, even when they lack an explicit cuboid trajectory in the conditioning signal. Closed-Loop Evaluation central application of OmniDreams is to act as generative sensor simulator inside closed-loop AV evaluation stack, as shown in Fig. 12. We pair it with NVIDIA AlpaSim (NVIDIA, 2025) as the simulation orchestrator (Sec. 6) and Alpamayo 1.5 (NVIDIA, 2026) as the driving policy, so that each closed-loop step alternates between policy action and OmniDreams sensor synthesis. We ask two concrete questions in this setup. First (Sec. 9.4.1), how does the generative simulator compare against reconstruction-based one when both drive the same policy through the same closed-loop stack? Second (Sec. 7), are the representations OmniDreams has learned during generative pre-training themselves useful for drivingi.e. can the same backbone be fine-tuned into competitive policy? 9.4.1. Closed-Loop Comparison to Reconstruction-based NuRec Simulator We swap only the sensor simulator between NVIDIA NuRec (NVIDIA, 2024), the reconstruction-based simulator that AlpaSim ships with by default, and OmniDreams; the details of the AlpaSim integration are described in Sec. 6. The orchestrator (AlpaSim), the traffic and physics services, and the per-scene initial state are held fixed; differences in the resulting rollout metrics therefore isolate the sensor simulators influence on policy behavior. To check that this conclusion is not specific to single policy, we sweep across multiple policy classes rather than fixing one. Visual Realism We further measure the visual realism of each simulator on the trajectories that matter for closed-loop evaluation, using (1) the ground-truth recorded trajectory (the ego path is aligned to the real video), (2) Alpamayo trajectory in NuRec simulator and (3) Alpamayo trajectory in OmniDreams simulator. NuRec-based simulator degrade quickly when the trajectory deviates from the ground truth trajectory whereas OmniDreams simulator maintains consistent visual realism. This is precisely where reconstruction-based renderers are stressed: novel viewpoints, changes in optical flow, and shifted occlusion boundaries require extrapolating beyond the captured rays. In contrast, OmniDreams can use its learned video prior and world-scenario conditioning to synthesize plausible observations beyond the recorded camera path. At the same time, OmniDreams only needs calibrated first-frame seed and the abstract world-scenario representation which can be produced by the AV data pipeline (Sec. 2.1, Sec. 2.2). One advantage is that the world-scenario map exposes the controllability and incident-coverage handles described in Sec. 9.3.1 that reconstruction-based simulator cannot natively provide. However, video-generation based simulator like OmniDreams natively requires much more compute than reconstruction-based simulator, exposing tradeoff between quality and compute. 10. Related Work Apart from the policy model itself, closed-loop autonomous-vehicle simulation primarily relies on two key components: world model that synthesizes sensor observations at requested position and time, and simulation infrastructure that orchestrates the simulation loop and connects the policy to the world model. We organize the related work around these two axes, focusing on the topics most relevant to our approach. First, we discuss world models, including reconstruction-based neural simulators, general video generative models, and generative driving models. Second, we review streaming and orchestration infrastructure for video-diffusion-based simulation. Together, these areas provide the technical context for the design choices underlying OmniDreams. 10.1. World models for closed-loop AV Simulation 10.1.1. Reconstruction-based World Models Reconstructing simulation environments from captured sensor data provides fast and scalable alternative to traditional artist-generated environments. By recovering scenes directly from real-world observations, this approach reduces the simulation-to-reality domain gap, scales with the amount of captured data rather than the number of artists, and enables rapid turnaround from on-road events to simulation-ready environments. NeRF-(Mildenhall et al., 2020; Müller et al., 2022) and 3D Gaussian Splatting (Kerbl et al., 2023; MoenneLoccoz et al., 2024; Wu et al., 2025) based methods have become dominant approaches for high-fidelity neural reconstruction of captured driving scenarios. NeuRAD (Tonderski et al., 2024) is one of the first works 26 NVIDIA OmniDreams: Real-Time Generative World Model for Closed-Loop Autonomous Vehicle Simulation Figure 15: Qualitative comparison to NuRec. NuRec renders the scene from per-scene reconstruction, while OmniDreams generates the camera observation from first-frame seed and abstract world scenario. OmniDreams preserves key driving cues such as lane geometry, traffic participants, and scene layout while improving pedestrian quality and producing more natural pedestrian motion that is difficult for reconstructionbased simulators to capture. that explicitly modeled camera and lidar sensor characteristics (rolling shutter, beam divergence, ray drop) across five automotive datasets, and EmerNeRF (Yang et al., 2024) introduces self-supervised decomposition of dynamic scenes into static, dynamic, and flow fields. SiMUli Turki et al. (2026) extends 3DGUT to support LiDAR rendering within 3D Gaussian Splatting formulations and addresses cross-sensor inconsistencies through factorized 3D Gaussian representation. NVIDIA Omniverse NuRec (NVIDIA, 2024) commercializes 3DGSbased reconstruction at production scale and serves as the renderer used by AlpaSim (NVIDIA, 2025) prior to the integration described in Sec. 6. Reconstruction-based World Models synthesize faithful sensor observations within captured corridors, making them well suited for what-if testing within logged scenes. However, they extrapolate poorly to content, viewpoints, and conditions absent from the original capture, motivating the generative direction we pursue here. 10.1.2. Video models as world simulator complementary line of work frames large, general-purpose video-generation models as world simulators trained on broad, internet-scale visual data. Sora (OpenAI, 2024) popularized this framing for diffusion-based video models; Movie Gen (Polyak et al., 2024) extends it to longer durations and broader controllability; Wan (Wan et al., 2025) releases an open large-scale video generative model; and Veo 3 (DeepMind, 2025) adds synchronized audio and speech. Genie (Bruce et al., 2024) takes different framing, learning foundation world model from unlabeled internet videos that supports action-controllable interactive rollouts from single image, text, or sketch prompt. The NVIDIA Cosmos world-foundation-model platform (Agarwal et al., 2025; NVIDIA, 2025) consolidates this line of work for the physical-AI setting and provides the backbone we post-train on. OmniDreams specializes Cosmos to the autonomous-driving setting: it adds action conditioning on policy trajectories and world-scenario-map control, runs in closed loop with real policy and traffic simulator, and meets per-chunk latency budget that the general-purpose models in this subsection do not target. 27 NVIDIA OmniDreams: Real-Time Generative World Model for Closed-Loop Autonomous Vehicle Simulation 10.1.3. Generative AV World Models parallel line of work uses video generation to synthesize driving footage conditioned on scene-level signals. DriveGAN (Kim et al., 2021) was the first generative controllable driving system trained entirely from real-world driving logs. More recently, DriveDreamer (Wang et al., 2024) introduced diffusion-based generative system for driving scenes; GAIA-1 (Hu et al., 2023) and its multi-view successor GAIA-2 (Russell et al., 2025) introduce structured controllability over weather, agents, ego dynamics, and surround-view camera rigs; Vista (Gao et al., 2024) emphasizes high-resolution generalization and multi-modal trajectory control; MagicDrive (Gao et al., 2024) and Drive-WM (Wang et al., 2024) target multi-view generation with explicit 3D-geometry and BEV-style controls; and GenAD (Yang et al., 2024) trains generalist video predictor on 2000+-hour open-domain corpus. Closer to our setting, the NVIDIA Cosmos world-foundation-model platform (NVIDIA, 2025) provides the backbone we post-train, and Cosmos-Drive-Dreams (Ren et al., 2025) shows that Cosmos-derived synthetic driving data improves downstream perception and policy training. Concurrently, Waymo introduced the Waymo World Model (Waymo, 2026), which adapts Genie-style foundation world model (Bruce et al., 2024) to autonomous driving and generates controllable, multi-sensor (camera and lidar) rollouts conditioned on driving inputs, scene layouts, and language prompts. OmniDreams differs from this line on three axes: (i) it is built for closed-loop interactivity rather than offline rollout; (ii) it integrates streaming KV cache and few-step distillation that drive per-chunk latency into the 100+ FPS regime (Sec. 5); and (iii) it ships with published microservice simulator (AlpaSim, Sec. 6) and published policy stack (Alpamayo 1 (NVIDIA, 2026)). 10.1.4. Real-time and streaming video diffusion Pushing per-chunk latency of video diffusion into interactive territory requires progress on both the algorithm and systems sides. Self Forcing (Huang et al., 2025) and CausVid (Yin et al., 2025) bridge the traintest gap of autoregressive video diffusion via self-rollout and asymmetric distillation; we adopt the Self Forcing recipe in Sec. 4 and combine it with distribution-matching distillation (DMD) (Yin et al., 2024). Long-rollout stability is the focus of StreamingT2V (Henschel et al., 2025) and FIFO-Diffusion (Kim et al., 2024), and Diffusion Forcing (Chen et al., 2024) provides the per-token noise schedule that underpins our causal mid-training. Our streaming-attention design extends the attention-sink construction from StreamingLLM (Xiao et al., 2024) to video-diffusion setting; ring attention (Liu et al., 2024) supplies the kernel pattern for our context-parallel attention; and we discuss future extensions to sparse temporal attention (Yuan et al., 2025) and lightweight super-resolution (Zhuang et al., 2025). 10.2. Closed-loop AV simulation infrastructure Simulator design for AV research has historically operated at one of three levels of perception realism: non-visual or simple abstract graphics, usually targeting massively parallel simulation for large scale RL training, such as Waymax (Gulino et al., 2023) and PufferDrive (Cornelisse et al., 2025), physical and visual realism obtained through artist-generated assets, exemplified by CARLA (Dosovitskiy et al., 2017) or MetaDrive (Li et al., 2022), graphics derived from neural reconstruction methods, marrying data-driven scenario generation with high visual quality: DriveArena (Yang et al., 2024), HUGSIM (Zhou et al., 2024), WorldEngine (OpenDriveLab, 2026) and AlpaSim (NVIDIA, 2025). We believe world models open new, 4th tier of perception realism. We demonstrate it by extending AlpaSim, as it easily admits remote, distributed video-model rendering thanks to its unique microservice-based architecture. Sec. 6 describes how we extend AlpaSim to support the chunk-based stateful semantics required by videodiffusion world model. 28 NVIDIA OmniDreams: Real-Time Generative World Model for Closed-Loop Autonomous Vehicle Simulation 11. Conclusion The transition from purely reconstruction-based environments to generative world models marks pivotal advancement in validating autonomous driving systems. By seamlessly integrating OmniDreams with the Alpamayo 1 policy and AlpaSim orchestrator, this framework demonstrates robust pathway for generating highly dynamic, unconstrained testing scenarios. Because the model effectively synthesizes complex, interactive elements, ranging from rare weather anomalies to unpredictable pedestrian movements, it can directly address the long-tail edge cases that standard simulators struggle to recreate. Ultimately, this generative, closed-loop approach equips developers with the scalable tools necessary to rigorously evaluate and potentially train advanced vision-language-action policies, effectively bridging the gap between simulated testing and safe real-world deployment. 29 NVIDIA OmniDreams: Real-Time Generative World Model for Closed-Loop Autonomous Vehicle Simulation A. Glossary This whitepaper draws on terminology from autonomous-driving research, video diffusion modeling, parallelattention systems, and the NVIDIA SIL platform stack. Acronyms and product names are listed below for reference; each term is also expanded on first mention in the body. Concepts and acronyms AV autonomous vehicle. VLA vision-language-action model. multimodal foundation model that ingests images or video plus text and emits an action (a planned trajectory or control signal). WAM World-Action Model. video-conditioned policy that maps video input to actions without an explicit language modality. VLM vision-language model. BEV birds-eye view; the top-down 2D ego-centric scene representation used by perception and trajectory pipelines. HD map high-definition map; vector representation of lanes, signs, crosswalks, and dynamic-agent bounding boxes used here as structured conditioning signal. VRU vulnerable road user; pedestrians, cyclists, scooter riders, and similar non-vehicle agents. LiDAR light detection and ranging; an active depth-sensing modality, referenced here in the context of neural-reconstruction baselines. DiT diffusion transformer; the architecture family that backs OmniDreams-SV and OmniDreams-MV. VAE variational autoencoder; the tokenizer that maps between pixels and the DiTs latent space. MLP multi-layer perceptron; used for the lightweight control-token encoder and the per-layer projections in the DiT. AdaLN adaptive layer normalization; conditioning is injected as an additive shift, scale, and gate modulation of normalized activations. RoPE rotary position embedding; relative positional encoding applied in the attention layers, with frequencies shared across denoising steps. KV cache key-value cache used by autoregressive transformers to amortize attention across steps; central to streaming inference (Sec. 5.1). Streaming KV cache bounded, in-place KV cache with static shapes that supports CUDA-graph capture and rolling-window eviction. Attention-sink tokens small fixed prefix of tokens kept in the KV cache to preserve long-range stability under local-window attention. Local-window attention temporal attention restricted to fixed-length recent window of chunks, giving constant per-chunk attention cost. Cross-view attention attention applied across simultaneously-generated camera views at each time step to enforce inter-view consistency. Context parallelism sharding strategy that splits sequence across GPUs along the spatial, temporal, or view axis; Sec. 5.2 introduces hierarchical 𝑉 𝑇 𝐻𝑊 factorization. 36 NVIDIA OmniDreams: Real-Time Generative World Model for Closed-Loop Autonomous Vehicle Simulation Ring attention context-parallel attention kernel that pipelines key-value shard exchanges against the local attention computation (24). Diffusion Forcing training paradigm in which each token receives an independent random noise level, enabling single model to act both as next-token predictor and as full-sequence denoiser (5). Self Forcing training paradigm that closes the autoregressive train-test gap by performing self-rollout during training, conditioning each frame on its own previously-generated outputs (16). DMD Distribution Matching Distillation (61); the holistic, video-level distribution-matching objective used in our distillation stage. KL KullbackLeibler divergence; the distillation objective minimizes (reverse) KL divergence between the model and data distributions. Rectified flow training objective used by the base Cosmos backbone (25; 33). Pixel-shuffle (HD map encoder) parameter-free patch-wise encoder that produces tokens at the same spatial and temporal resolution as the RGB branch, replacing learned 3D VAE for the HD map signal. TAE fast VAE decoder from LightTAE. CUDA Graphs an NVIDIA execution-model construct that lets the entire DiT forward pass be replayed as single GPU submission (31). Pre-fetch / post-fetch two strategies for integrating chunk-based generation with closed-loop simulation; Sec. 6 adopts pre-fetch for AlpaSim. gRPC, NCCL, RDMA communication primitives used by AlpaSim for cross-process coordination, by the multiGPU video model for collective ops, and as future direction for high-throughput frame transport. ControlNet control-adapter pattern for diffusion models, included here as contrast to the lightweight control branch we use. FVD Fréchet Video Distance; distribution-level video-quality metric referenced in Sec. 9. LET-AP / LET-APL / LET-APH Longitudinal-Error-Tolerant Average Precision and its localization- (APL) and heading-weighted (APH) variants; 3D object-detection metrics reported in Sec. 9, computed with an off-the-shelf detector (17). NVIDIA SIL platform components SIL NVIDIA Spatial Intelligence Lab. Cosmos / Cosmos-Predict 2.5 NVIDIAs video-foundation-model platform (33); OmniDreams-SV and OmniDreamsMV post-train from Cosmos-Predict 2.5. OmniDreams the system documented in this whitepaper. OmniDreams-SV / OmniDreams-MV the single-view and multi-view variants described in Sec. 3. Cosmos-Drive-Dreams NVIDIAs synthetic-driving-data generation pipeline (42), complementary to OmniDreams. Alpamayo / Alpamayo 1 NVIDIAs open-source AV policy stack (36); the policy that drives the closed-loop demos in Sec. 6. AlpaSim NVIDIAs open-source closed-loop AV simulator (34); the orchestrator into which OmniDreams plugs in Sec. 6. 37 NVIDIA OmniDreams: Real-Time Generative World Model for Closed-Loop Autonomous Vehicle Simulation NuRec / Omniverse NuRec NVIDIAs neural-reconstruction tool for AV simulation (30); the reconstructionbased renderer that NuRec replaces in our integration. SIL-Wheel NVIDIA SILs multi-modal AV video search and curation platform (37); used to construct training and evaluation slices for the experiments in Sec. 2 and 9. See Sec. 2.5. GB300 NVIDIA Grace Blackwell platforms; Sec. 5 reports per-chunk latency on single GB300 and 16-GPU GB300 cabinet. Datasets and benchmarks RDS the multi-view AV training corpus used for mid-training; sourced from real-world driving logs at 1080p,

The update direction is 𝑓𝜓 𝑓𝜑 (Sec. 4). (training-section meaning) the training loss, e.g. L𝐷𝐹 = Ex1:𝑇 ,𝜖 training. [u𝜃(x1:𝑇 , t) vt2] for Diffusion Forcing Sizes and parallelism axes. 𝑇 (sequence meaning) number of latent frames in generation chunk or sequence. Overload: in Sec. 5.2, 𝑇 denotes the temporal-axis context-parallelism degree. Overload: in Sec. 4, 𝐾 is the number of denoising steps in the Self Forcing self-rollout, with 𝐾=2 at the timestep schedule [1000, 450].
